title_en: An-Nawawi and His Commentary on Sahih Muslim
content_en:
Abu Zakariyya Yahya ibn Sharaf an-Nawawi was born in 631 AH in the village of Nawa in the Hawran region of Syria, from which his nisba (attributive name) is derived. He came to Damascus as a young man to pursue his education and spent essentially the rest of his short life there, studying, teaching, and writing at a pace that produced an output remarkable for its quality and breadth. Despite living only forty-four years and never having married or accumulated wealth — he lived with extreme simplicity — he left behind a body of scholarly work that has defined Islamic scholarship in the Shafi'i tradition for seven and a half centuries.

An-Nawawi studied the full range of Islamic sciences but his most lasting contributions were in hadith, Islamic law, and what might be called the integration of these disciplines into the practical religious life of the believer. His Al-Minhaj Sharh Sahih Muslim — The Methodology: A Commentary on Sahih Muslim — is his most extensive single work and one of the most important commentaries on any hadith collection ever written. It represents the mature synthesis of an extraordinary scholar who brought linguistic precision, legal depth, theological carefulness, and spiritual insight to bear on the text of Imam Muslim's great collection.

An-Nawawi approached the project of commenting on Sahih Muslim with a set of clear scholarly purposes: to explain the linguistic content of the hadiths for students whose Arabic was not at the level of the classical sources; to derive and discuss the legal rulings implied by each tradition; to address the theological dimensions of the material, particularly where it touched on questions of divine attributes and the nature of the afterlife; and to note points of agreement and disagreement among the legal schools on matters arising from each hadith. This fourfold purpose gives Al-Minhaj its characteristic thoroughness.

An-Nawawi completed the commentary and died in 676 AH, just a few years after finishing it. His other major works — Riyadh as-Salihin, Al-Adhkar, the Forty Hadiths, and his legal writings — were all composed within the same brief but extraordinarily productive scholarly life. The influence of Al-Minhaj on subsequent scholarship has been immense and continues to shape how Muslim scholars and students engage with Sahih Muslim.
